name: South Korea
summary: South Korea is a global technological powerhouse and a leading liberal democracy in East Asia. After a period of significant political upheaval in late 2024, the nation has transitioned to a new administration under President Lee Jae-myung, focusing on pragmatic diplomacy and economic revitalization. South Korea remains a critical node in global supply chains, particularly in semiconductors and high-tech manufacturing. However, it faces severe structural challenges, most notably the world's lowest fertility rate and an rapidly aging population, which pose long-term risks to its economic dynamism and social stability.

politicalStability.note: Recovering from the late 2024 political crisis; democratic institutions proved resilient during the transition.

economicDirection.note: Strong high-tech exports continue to drive growth, though domestic consumption is hampered by high household debt.

investmentAttractiveness.note: Aggressive government incentives for AI and chips; however, regulatory complexity and labor rigidity remain concerns.

geopoliticalRisk.note: Persistent tensions with North Korea and a complex 'North Korea-Russia-China' axis; deepening trilateral ties with US and Japan.

educationQuality.note: One of the world's most educated workforces; extremely high emphasis on STEM and higher education.

healthcareQuality.note: Excellent, efficient universal healthcare system; a global leader in medical technology and pharmaceuticals.

technologyInvestment.note: World-class R&D spending; national priority on semiconductors, AI, and green energy technology.

relationships.summary: Core security ally; relationship is deepening in technology and supply chain security despite cost-sharing pressures.
Relationship has significantly improved; focused on trilateral security cooperation to counter common threats.
Largest trade partner; relationship is carefully managed to balance economic needs with security concerns.
Direct existential threat; relations remain frozen with high military readiness on both sides.
Relations have deteriorated due to the Ukraine war and Russia's deepening military ties with North Korea.

perspectives.view: We must lead in AI and semiconductors to survive. Global supply chain shifts are our biggest challenge and our biggest opportunity.
The competition is suffocating, and the cost of living makes it impossible to start a family. We need more than just chip factories to have a future.
The threats are evolving. We need to maintain our technological edge and strengthen our alliances to ensure peace through strength.
Our success means nothing if we don't address the inequality and the demographic collapse. We need a fundamental shift in our social contract.

historicalContext.summary: Following the Korean War (1950-1953), South Korea underwent rapid industrialization known as the 'Miracle on the Han River.' It transitioned from a military dictatorship to a vibrant democracy in 1987. It is now a leading global economy and a key cultural exporter (Hallyu).

keyEvents.event: Establishment of the Republic of Korea.
Korean Armistice Agreement signed, establishing the DMZ.
Park Chung-hee takes power in a military coup, initiating rapid economic development.
June Democracy Movement leads to direct presidential elections and the Sixth Republic.
Asian Financial Crisis leads to significant IMF-mandated structural reforms.
Impeachment of President Park Geun-hye after mass protests.
Political crisis following a brief martial law declaration; leads to the impeachment of President Yoon Suk-yeol.

strengths: Global leader in Semiconductors and Display technology
Strong R&D environment and government support for innovation
risks: High household debt and slowing domestic consumption
Geopolitical volatility on the Korean Peninsula
Regulatory barriers and Chaebol-dominated market structure
